# Copilot instructions — SerialCommunication

## Quick build / run commands

- Build (Visual Studio / MSBuild)
  - msbuild "SerialCommunication.slnx" /p:Configuration=Debug
  - Open SerialCommunication.slnx in Visual Studio and Build (recommended on Windows).
- Run executable: open SerialCommunication\bin\Debug (after build) and run the .exe.
- Arduino firmware compile/upload (arduino-cli)
  - Compile: arduino-cli compile --fqbn arduino:avr:uno "SerialCommunication.ino"
  - Upload: arduino-cli upload -p COM3 --fqbn arduino:avr:uno "SerialCommunication.ino"
  - Replace COM3 and fqbn with target board/port.
- Tests: none present in this repository.
- Linting: no linter configured. Use Visual Studio analyzers or add tooling if needed.

## How to run one-off checks

- "Run the app" means build solution then run the generated exe from bin\Debug.
- "Flash firmware" use arduino-cli upload to the device's COM port.

## High-level architecture

- Desktop app (C# WinForms) — folder: SerialCommunication
  - UI enumerates serial ports (System.IO.Ports.SerialPort), default baudrate 115200.
  - Program.cs launches Form1 which exposes controls to pick port/baud and send/receive messages.
- Firmware (Arduino sketch + helper files) — root:
  - SerialCommunication.ino implements command handlers (set, toggle, get, ping, help, debug).
  - SerialCommand.h/.cpp — a small command tokenizer/parser library. Commands are tokenized by spaces and terminated by a newline (term set to '\n' in the sketch).
  - analog.c provides analogReadDelay helper used by get a[0..5].
- Runtime interaction: the desktop UI opens the serial port at 115200 and issues ASCII commands like "set d2 high". The firmware replies with human-readable responses (e.g. "set done", "d3: 1", "a0: 512", "pong", or error messages).

## Protocol details (important to preserve)

- Terminator: newline ('\n') as defined in SerialCommand.cpp (term='\n').
- Token delimiter: single space (SerialCommand::delim default).
- Commands and example forms:
  - set d[2..4] [on|off|1|0|high|low]
    - Example: "set d2 high"
  - set pwm[9..11] [0..255]
    - Example: "set pwm9 128"
  - toggle d[2..4]
    - Example: "toggle d3"
  - get d[2..7]
    - Example: "get d4" → replies like "d4: 1"
  - get a[0..5]
    - Example: "get a2" → replies like "a2: 512"
  - ping → replies "pong"
  - debug, help
- Baudrate: 115200 (SerialCommunication.ino).
- Buffer limits: SERIALCOMMANDBUFFER = 32, MAXSERIALCOMMANDS = 10 — avoid sending very long single commands or increasing these without re-evaluating memory impact on AVR.

## Key conventions and patterns

- Firmware strings use the F() macro to keep flash memory usage low.
- The Arduino code uses simple ASCII, human-readable responses — CI or tooling that parses messages should match exact prefixes (e.g. 'd', 'a', or plain messages like "set done").
- The C# UI currently only scaffolds port enumeration and selection; the connect/send implementation lives in Form1.* (buttonConnect is currently a stub).
- When modifying commands, update both firmware command handlers and any desktop code that sends or parses responses.

## Files and areas to inspect when changing behavior

- SerialCommand.h / SerialCommand.cpp — parser/tokenizer and constants.
- SerialCommunication.ino — command handlers, pin ranges, and baudrate.
- analog.c — analog read helper (timing/averaging behavior).
- SerialCommunication\Form1.cs and Form1.Designer.cs — UI and serial I/O glue (send/receive logic).

## Found AI assistant configs

- No CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, AGENTS.md, .windsurfrules, CONVENTIONS.md, or other AI assistant rule files were found in the repository root. If you have project-specific assistant rules, add them under the repository root and this document will be updated.

## Notes for Copilot sessions

- Preserve the exact ASCII protocol (terminator and tokenization) when generating code that interacts with firmware.
- Avoid changing SERIALCOMMANDBUFFER or MAXSERIALCOMMANDS without testing on hardware.
- When adding new commands, mirror names and response formats in both firmware and desktop code.
